lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Aristotle, we have mastered the logic of propositions — of statements that are simply true or false. But the world is richer than this.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: You mean we must speak of **things** — of objects and their properties? A horse is not merely a true or false thing; it has qualities.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Precisely! Consider: 'All men are mortal. Socrates is a man. Therefore, Socrates is mortal.' This reasoning cannot be captured by propositional logic alone.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: Because 'all men are mortal' speaks of every man — it quantifies over the domain of men. We need a way to say **for all** $x$, something holds of $x$.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Yes! That is the **[universal quantifier|universal quantifier]** $\forall x$. And its companion: **[existential quantifier|existential quantifier]** $\exists x$ — 'there exists some $x$ such that...'.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: So we can say $\forall x.\; \text{Man}(x) \to \text{Mortal}(x)$ — every man is mortal. And $\text{Man}(\text{Socrates})$ — Socrates is a man.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: And from these, we derive $\text{Mortal}(\text{Socrates})$. This is **[universal instantiation|universal instantiation]** — what holds for all holds for any particular. The command is `(forall-elim N t)`.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: And the reverse? If I prove something about an arbitrary $x$, I can generalise to $\forall x$?

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Exactly — **[universal generalisation|universal generalisation]**. But be careful: the variable must be **fresh** — not appearing free in any assumption. Otherwise you'd be proving something about a specific $x$ and claiming it holds for all.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: And for the existential $\exists$? If I have a concrete example, I can say 'there exists something with this property' — [existential generalisation|existential generalisation].

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Yes. And the trickier one: if you know $\exists x.\; \varphi$, you can **open a witness** — assume $\varphi[y/x]$ for a fresh $y$, prove your goal, and [discharge|discharge] the witness. This is **[existential elimination|existential elimination]**.

lines.speaker: aristotle
lines.text: So first-order logic gives us predicates, terms, and quantifiers — a language rich enough to capture the [syllogism|syllogism] and much more.

lines.speaker: plato
lines.text: Indeed. And you will need a new command: `(fix x)` introduces a fresh term variable, like `(assume F)` but for terms. Now, let us quantify.
